0.flavor: 1995. CRT phosphor monitor. My first command prompt.

bio.core: Rafael Matovelle — full stack + QA, gamedev roots, three decades of tech. And always with a smile. 😄

0.text: It all started with a DOS machine and seven years of age. While other kids were discovering cartoons, I was discovering California Games, Stunts, and Out of This World. Those pixels were my first love. Then came Warcraft, StarCraft, and Magic: The Gathering, which taught me that behind every digital world there was someone building it. And I wanted to be that someone.

1.text:
It started as a punishment for nearly getting expelled from school and ended up becoming my vocation. My cousin handed me my first HTML/JS/CSS book so I wouldn't get bored during summer break when I was 14 — a summer I spent grounded, banned from everything except reading and using the computer to work through the book's exercises.

At 14, I grabbed a book, opened a text editor, and taught myself to code. I started with HTML, JavaScript, and CSS, building my own websites. Then I jumped to Perl, and from there got hooked on C++. Each language was a new world, another way of thinking. For me, programming has always been another flavor of Legos: pieces that fit together, worlds that appear out of nothing.

In parallel (2002–2006), I lived another life: as a professional StarCraft and Warcraft III player. Four years of serious competition taught me things no course can: reading systems in real time, finding the pattern before your opponent does, handling pressure.

And that world opened my first job: I got in as a Game Tester at BlueLizard Games (BLG). My first paid gig was, literally, QA.

2.text:
From tester to programmer. At BlueLizard, Matte CG, and Joju Games I worked as a gameplay programmer during the golden age of Flash. I worked on titles like Spamania 2, Club Paradise 2, Lost Realms 2, Megamind Amazing Machine, and Nanoland. Then came Bingo Blingo on Facebook, and a bunch of advergames for Coca-Cola, Marlboro, and Mulgatol.

It was a brutal school. I learned that making games is 10% magic and 90% figuring out why the character is walking through the wall again. And advergames taught me something extra: how to fit a brand inside a mechanic without killing the fun.

3.text:
Flash's death looked like just that: an ending. I had grown up with it — it was what I used and, in many ways, who I was for years. But I soon realized it wasn't a burial but a leap: HTML5 and JavaScript arrived to bring motion back to the web, and to bring me back to it. Back to JS, back to the interactive web.

I was riding through Pink Parrot somewhere between excitement and chaos, in a time when everything was changing at once — the technology and my own career. Animations that used to live inside the plugin now had to be rebuilt from scratch, under new rules nobody had fully mastered. I learned a lot out of necessity, and that's the best way to learn.

It was in that mess that agile stopped being slide-deck theory and became a way of thinking. Sprints were the difference between shipping and sinking; retros were the moment to be honest about what hadn't worked. More than a process, it became how I made sense of the chaos and grew as a professional.

Digital advertising in that era still surprised users. There was room to experiment with interactivity, to build things that felt new because they actually were. I worked right on that edge between communication and code, between what a brand wanted to say and what the web was finally capable of doing.

It was a time to grow on every front. Not everything worked out, but everything left something behind — and what looked like the death of Flash turned out to be my leap back to the interactive web.

4.text:
I started an independent venture working with Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality applied to BTL advertising. Three years building things almost nobody else was doing yet in Ecuador: brand experiences where the user was part of the game, not a spectator.

Then came Metrodigi, and with it my first real phase as a leader. Prioritizing, unblocking, protecting the team from noise, and still coding. This stage taught me something key: I love the moment when an idea goes from "what if…?" to "look, it works," and I love it even more when it's the whole team that gets there.

5.text:
At number8 I led QA as Lead Software QA Engineer for almost three years, working with AWS and building processes from scratch. Then at BairesDev I joined the R&D team to do data science test automation, all in Python. It was a different game: not validating clicks or forms, but pipelines measuring models, datasets, and statistical behavior — bugs that look more like biases, drifts, and edge cases than classic logic errors. Here I discovered something that changed how I see software: quality isn't something you check at the end, it's something you build in from the start.

In parallel (2019–2024) I was co-owner of VivoEnVivo, a streaming platform for events and contact sports in Ecuador. Five years learning what it's like to build something of your own from zero.

At RocketSnail I led the frontend, creating a custom framework with Vue and Lottie. Then at Remoose Interactive I worked as a Full Stack Engineer and rapid prototyper on the R&D team. Vue, Node.js, WebGL, the whole stack.

6.text:
And since late 2023 I've been at Software Mind, applying automation and tooling to data science. And now AI and agents give me something I've been waiting for since I was seven years old playing on DOS: the ability to build everything I always wanted, automate it, and prototype at the speed of imagination.

Looking back, everything fits together in a strange-but-right way. The competitive years gave me the systems mindset. Flash games gave me the speed of iteration. Advergames taught me how to tell stories inside code. The web gave me the canvas and agile. AR/VR taught me to prototype the impossible. Leading teams taught me that code alone doesn't build anything big. QA gave me rigor. Data science gave me the data.

Every stage looked like a leap. Seen from far enough away, it was a single line.
